summary: Gruber methodically works through the math of possible display resolutions for the rumored 4.7-inch and 5.5-inch iPhone 6 models. He argues that Apple's design constraints around virtual points, touch target sizes, and pixels-per-inch density severely limit the viable options. His conclusion: the 4.7-inch model will use 1334×750 at @2× (maintaining the existing 326 PPI), while the 5.5-inch model will introduce @3× retina scaling at 2208×1242 (461 PPI). He systematically eliminates other candidates like 1704×960, 1472×828, and 1920×1080 by showing how they fail on scaling factor, point density, or content area.
key_points: Apple's UI is built on virtual points, not pixels, and the recommended 44-point tap target constrains what display resolutions are viable — points per inch cannot increase without making touch targets too small
The 4.7-inch iPhone sweet spot is 1334×750 @2× because it maintains exactly 326 PPI and 163 points per inch while adding 38% more content area
The 5.5-inch model demands @3× retina scaling at 2208×1242, which would improve all three factors: 68% more content, 6% larger points, and 461 PPI sharpness
Simply tripling the iPhone 5's resolution (1704×960) would show no additional content in points, making it pointless for larger screens
Large-screen phones need to address two distinct desires — showing more content AND making content physically larger — and the best resolutions balance both
money_quotes: There is no similar 'easy' way to do either a 4.7 or 5.5 iPhone display.
No one who is truly 'familiar with the situation' has told me a damn thing about either device.
What they have never done, and I believe never will do, is redefine the virtual point to something other than 1/44th the recommended minimum tap target size for every device.
This recommendation is based not on aesthetics — how the controls look — but on the size of human fingertips. That's the real constraint.
Dynamic Type alone is not a good enough solution to showing more content — the only good solution is increasing the number of points, not just the number of pixels.
At 461 pixels-per-inch, everything would be amazingly sharp. At that point it would be difficult for most of us to perceive individual pixels from any viewing distance.
This is why it's essential to consider points, not just pixels.
@3× is going to be a bit of a pain in the ass for pixel-obsessive designers.
key_insight: Apple's display choices are constrained not by pixel counts but by the physics of fingertips — the virtual point system that ensures consistent touch target sizes dictates which resolutions are viable for new screen sizes.
